message:
chore(beads): require /porting-to-rust for Ghostty clean room implementations

Update wa-3bja epic and wa-3bja.5 synthesis to mandate:
- /porting-to-rust skill for all Zig→Rust implementations
- Essence extraction methodology (spec first, not line-by-line)
- Idiomatic memory-safe Rust, no unsafe unless provably necessary
- Clean room: write from language-neutral specs, not from Zig source

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

files.patch:
@@ -493,12 +493,12 @@
 {"id":"wa-3axa","title":"Custom Allocator (jemalloc + Per-Pane Arenas)","description":"## Goal
Replace the WezTerm mux server's default allocator with jemalloc configured for reduced fragmentation, and add per-pane arena allocation for terminal cell data, directly attacking the memory leak root cause.

## Background & Motivation
The mux server's 76GB RSS after 23 days is partially caused by memory fragmentation. The default system allocator (glibc malloc on Linux) fragments under WezTerm's allocation pattern:
- Many small allocations (terminal cells: ~24 bytes each)
- Frequent allocation/deallocation cycles (scrollback trimming)
- Long-lived allocations mixed with short-lived ones
- Multiple threads allocating concurrently (2 threads per pane)

jemalloc's thread-local caches and size-class bins dramatically reduce fragmentation for this pattern. Per-pane arenas further isolate allocation, allowing entire pane teardown to reclaim memory instantly.

## Technical Design

### Phase 1: jemalloc Global Allocator
```rust
// In wezterm-mux-server/src/main.rs
#[global_allocator]
static ALLOC: tikv_jemallocator::Jemalloc = tikv_jemallocator::Jemalloc;

// Configure via MALLOC_CONF environment variable:
// "background_thread:true,dirty_decay_ms:5000,muzzy_decay_ms:30000"
```

jemalloc configuration tuned for terminal workload:
- `background_thread:true` — return memory to OS asynchronously
- `dirty_decay_ms:5000` — release dirty pages after 5s (vs default 10s)
- `muzzy_decay_ms:30000` — release muzzy pages after 30s
- `narenas:4` — limit arena count to reduce fragmentation spread

### Phase 2: Per-Pane Arena (Bumpalo)
```rust
use bumpalo::Bump;

pub struct PaneAllocator {
    /// Arena for terminal cell data (frequently allocated/freed)
    cell_arena: Bump,
    /// Allocation count for monitoring
    alloc_count: AtomicU64,
    /// Total bytes allocated
    total_bytes: AtomicU64,
}

impl PaneAllocator {
    /// Reset arena (instant deallocation of all pane data)
    pub fn reset(&mut self) {
        self.cell_arena.reset();
        self.alloc_count.store(0, Ordering::Relaxed);
        self.total_bytes.store(0, Ordering::Relaxed);
    }
}
```

When a pane is closed, its entire arena is freed in O(1) — no fragmentation possible.

### Phase 3: Allocation Telemetry
```rust
// jemalloc stats integration
pub fn allocation_stats() -> AllocStats {
    let mut stats = jemalloc_ctl::stats::allocated::read().unwrap();
    AllocStats {
        allocated: stats,
        resident: jemalloc_ctl::stats::resident::read().unwrap(),
        retained: jemalloc_ctl::stats::retained::read().unwrap(),
        fragmentation_ratio: 1.0 - (stats as f64 / resident as f64),
    }
}
```

### ESO Methodology
1. BASELINE: Record RSS curve with default allocator under 50-pane load
2. PROFILE: heaptrack comparison (default vs jemalloc)
3. PROVE: Golden output checksums unchanged
4. IMPLEMENT: Phase 1 first (global jemalloc), measure, then Phase 2 (arenas)
5. VERIFY: RSS growth rate < 1MB/hour

### Implementation Location
- Modify: wezterm-mux-server Cargo.toml (add tikv-jemallocator)
- Modify: wezterm-mux-server main.rs (set global allocator)
- New: frankenterm-core/src/pane_allocator.rs (per-pane arenas)

## Dependencies
- wa-3kxe.1 (memory leak analysis): identifies which allocations benefit most from arenas
- FrankenTerm fork (bd-20fw): modifying mux server code

## Acceptance Criteria
- jemalloc as global allocator with tuned configuration
- Per-pane arena allocation for terminal cell data
- Arena reset on pane close (O(1) deallocation)
- Allocation telemetry via jemalloc stats
- RSS growth rate < 1MB/hour (measured over 24h)
- No behavior change (golden output verification)
- Benchmark: RSS curve comparison default vs jemalloc vs jemalloc+arenas","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-09T22:43:47.055141Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-02-09T23:16:58.211453Z","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"wa-3axa","depends_on_id":"wa-3kxe","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-09T22:45:30.21578Z","created_by":"jemanuel"},{"issue_id":"wa-3axa","depends_on_id":"wa-3kxe.1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-09T22:45:51.525511Z","created_by":"jemanuel"},{"issue_id":"wa-3axa","depends_on_id":"bd-20fw","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-09T23:16:58.211356Z","created_by":"jemanuel"}]}

 {"id":"wa-3bin","title":"Smart pane priority classification — intelligent resource allocation","description":"## Goal
Implement intelligent pane priority classification that uses wa's pattern detection engine to identify which panes contain the most important activity, enabling priority-based resource allocation across the capture and monitoring pipeline.

## Background & Motivation
Not all panes are equal. In a 50+ pane swarm:
- 2-3 panes might be actively generating code changes (highest priority)
- 5-10 panes might be processing/thinking (medium priority)
- 10-15 panes might be waiting for rate limit cooldowns (low priority)
- The rest might be idle shells or completed tasks (minimal priority)

wa already has pattern detection (patterns.rs) that can identify agent states (idle, thinking, error, rate-limited, etc.). This bead turns those detections into priority classifications that drive resource allocation decisions across the entire pipeline.

## Technical Design

### Priority Model
```rust
// Location: crates/wa-core/src/priority.rs (new file)

#[derive(Debug, Clone, Copy, PartialEq, Eq, PartialOrd, Ord)]
pub enum PanePriority {
    Critical = 4,    // Active code changes, user interaction, errors needing attention
    High = 3,        // Actively producing output
    Medium = 2,      // Thinking/processing
    Low = 1,         // Idle, waiting
    Background = 0,  // Rate-limited, dormant, completed
}

pub struct PriorityClassifier {
    pattern_engine: Arc<PatternEngine>,
    output_rates: DashMap<PaneId, OutputRateTracker>,
    overrides: DashMap<PaneId, PanePriority>,  // Manual overrides
}
```

### OutputRateTracker with Exponential Decay
The output rate tracker uses exponential weighted moving average (EWMA) to avoid stale rates dominating classification. Recent output matters more than old output.

```rust
pub struct OutputRateTracker {
    /// EWMA of lines per second, with configurable half-life
    ewma_lines_per_sec: f64,
    /// Last time a rate sample was recorded
    last_sample: Instant,
    /// Half-life for exponential decay (default: 10 seconds)
    /// After 10s of silence, rate decays to 50%. After 30s, ~12.5%.
    half_life: Duration,
    /// Total lines observed (for lifetime stats)
    total_lines: u64,
}

impl OutputRateTracker {
    pub fn new(half_life: Duration) -> Self {
        Self {
            ewma_lines_per_sec: 0.0,
            last_sample: Instant::now(),
            half_life,
            total_lines: 0,
        }
    }

    /// Record new output lines and update EWMA
    pub fn record_output(&mut self, line_count: usize) {
        let now = Instant::now();
        let elapsed = now.duration_since(self.last_sample);

        // Apply exponential decay to existing rate
        let decay_factor = (-elapsed.as_secs_f64() * (2.0_f64.ln() / self.half_life.as_secs_f64())).exp();
        self.ewma_lines_per_sec *= decay_factor;

        // Add new sample contribution
        if elapsed.as_secs_f64() > 0.0 {
            let instant_rate = line_count as f64 / elapsed.as_secs_f64();
            let alpha = 1.0 - decay_factor;  // smoothing factor
            self.ewma_lines_per_sec += alpha * instant_rate;
        }

        self.last_sample = now;
        self.total_lines += line_count as u64;
    }

    /// Get current rate with decay applied for time since last sample
    pub fn lines_per_second(&self) -> f64 {
        let elapsed = Instant::now().duration_since(self.last_sample);
        let decay = (-elapsed.as_secs_f64() * (2.0_f64.ln() / self.half_life.as_secs_f64())).exp();
        self.ewma_lines_per_sec * decay
    }
}
```

### Classification Logic
```rust
impl PriorityClassifier {
    pub fn classify(&self, pane_id: PaneId) -> PanePriority {
        // Check manual override first
        if let Some(override_p) = self.overrides.get(&pane_id) {
            return *override_p;
        }

        let state = self.pattern_engine.current_state(pane_id);
        let rate = self.output_rates.get(&pane_id)
            .map(|r| r.lines_per_second())
            .unwrap_or(0.0);

        match state {
            AgentState::Error | AgentState::NeedsAttention => PanePriority::Critical,
            AgentState::Active if rate > 10.0 => PanePriority::High,
            AgentState::Active => PanePriority::Medium,
            AgentState::Thinking => PanePriority::Medium,
            AgentState::WaitingForInput => PanePriority::Low,
            AgentState::RateLimited => PanePriority::Background,
            AgentState::Idle => PanePriority::Background,
            _ => PanePriority::Low,
        }
    }
}
```

### Configuration
```toml
[priority]
rate_half_life_secs = 10    # EWMA half-life for output rate decay
high_rate_threshold = 10.0  # lines/sec to qualify as "High"
```

### Resource Allocation Consumers
- **CaptureScheduler** (tailer.rs): Poll interval based on priority (enhances tiered rates from bd-9dp)
- **Connection pool**: Priority queue for pool connections (high priority panes served first)
- **Backpressure** (backpressure.rs): Under pressure, shed Background first, then Low
- **Snapshot engine**: Capture high-priority pane scrollback first

### Robot Mode API
```bash
# View current priorities
wa robot priorities
# Override a pane's priority
wa robot priority set <pane_id> critical
# Reset to auto-classification
wa robot priority reset <pane_id>
```

## Dependencies
- bd-9dp (Tiered update rates): This bead provides the classification that drives the tier selection in bd-9dp

## Acceptance Criteria
- Panes classified into correct priorities based on agent state + output rate
- Output rate uses EWMA with exponential decay (half-life configurable, default 10s)
- After 30s of silence, rate decays to ~12.5% (correctly reflects inactivity)
- Manual overrides take precedence
- Priority changes propagate to CaptureScheduler within 1 second
- Robot Mode API for viewing and overriding priorities
- Metrics: pane count per priority level, rate distribution

## Estimated Effort
3-4 hours implementation, 1 hour testing","status":"open","priority":3,"issue_type":"feature","created_at":"2026-02-09T19:36:30.76596Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-02-09T21:08:15.73071Z","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"wa-3bin","depends_on_id":"bd-9dp","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-09T19:38:35.738449Z","created_by":"jemanuel"},{"issue_id":"wa-3bin","depends_on_id":"wa-3cyp","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-09T21:08:15.730558Z","created_by":"jemanuel"}]}
-{"id":"wa-3bja","title":"[EPIC] Mine Ghostty codebase for FrankenTerm improvement ideas","description":"## Goal
Clone the Ghostty terminal emulator codebase and systematically mine it for architectural ideas, performance techniques, and design patterns that can improve FrankenTerm (formerly wezterm_automata).

## Background & Motivation
Ghostty is a modern, high-performance terminal emulator written in Zig by Mitchell Hashimoto (creator of Vagrant, Terraform, Consul). It represents a fresh take on terminal emulator architecture with:
- GPU-accelerated rendering via custom font rasterization
- Zero-copy I/O pipeline
- Modern event loop design
- Clean separation of concerns
- Performance-first architecture

By studying Ghostty's codebase, we can identify techniques to apply to FrankenTerm's WezTerm integration layer, especially for:
1. **Memory efficiency**: How Ghostty handles scrollback (vs WezTerm's VecDeque<Line>)
2. **I/O pipeline**: How Ghostty reads PTY output (vs WezTerm's 2-thread-per-pane model)
3. **Event system**: How Ghostty handles notifications (vs WezTerm's RwLock fan-out)
4. **Configuration**: How Ghostty handles runtime config changes
5. **Mux architecture**: If/how Ghostty does multiplexing (vs WezTerm's singleton Mux)

## Approach
1. Clone Ghostty into legacy_ghostty_code/ (sibling to frankenterm/)
2. Read and annotate key architectural files
3. Create comparison documents: Ghostty approach vs WezTerm approach vs FrankenTerm opportunity
4. File specific improvement beads inspired by Ghostty patterns
5. Do NOT copy code directly — extract ideas and patterns, implement from scratch in Rust

## Sub-beads
1. Clone and inventory — get the codebase, map the architecture
2. Memory architecture analysis — scrollback, buffers, allocation patterns
3. I/O pipeline analysis — PTY reading, parsing, rendering pipeline
4. Event/notification system analysis — how changes propagate
5. Mux/multiplexing analysis — if it exists, how it works
6. Synthesis report — comparison doc with actionable FrankenTerm improvements

## Ethical Considerations
- Ghostty is open source (MIT license) — studying is fine
- We're extracting IDEAS and PATTERNS, not copying code
- Any inspired code will be original Rust implementations
- Credit Ghostty in commit messages where appropriate","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2026-02-09T19:37:22.248449Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-02-09T19:37:22.248449Z"}
+{"id":"wa-3bja","title":"[EPIC] Mine Ghostty codebase for FrankenTerm improvement ideas","description":"## Goal
Mine the Ghostty terminal emulator codebase for architectural ideas, performance techniques, and design patterns. Then do **clean room re-implementations** of the best ideas in idiomatic, memory-safe Rust using the **/porting-to-rust** skill.

## Background & Motivation
Ghostty is a modern, high-performance terminal emulator written in **Zig** by Mitchell Hashimoto (creator of Vagrant, Terraform, Consul). It represents a fresh take on terminal emulator architecture with:
- GPU-accelerated rendering via custom font rasterization
- Zero-copy I/O pipeline
- Modern event loop design
- Clean separation of concerns
- Performance-first architecture

## CRITICAL: Implementation methodology

### You MUST use the /porting-to-rust skill
When implementing any idea extracted from Ghostty, agents MUST invoke the **/porting-to-rust** skill. This skill enforces:
1. **Essence Extraction** — Distill the Zig code's spec/intent into a language-neutral specification FIRST
2. **Clean room implementation** — Write idiomatic Rust from the spec, NOT a line-by-line port of Zig
3. **Memory safety** — Leverage Rust's ownership model, lifetimes, and borrowing instead of Zig's manual memory patterns
4. **Idiomatic Rust** — Use Result/Option, iterators, traits, enums, and the type system fully. No C-in-Rust or Zig-in-Rust.
5. **No unsafe** — Unless provably necessary for FFI or performance, and then minimized and documented

### Why clean room matters
Ghostty's Zig code uses manual memory management, explicit allocators, and C-compatible patterns. A naive line-by-line translation would produce terrible Rust. The /porting-to-rust skill's essence extraction methodology ensures we get the IDEA (the algorithm, the architecture, the data flow) and re-express it as idiomatic Rust that a Rust developer would have designed from scratch.

## Areas to study
1. **Memory efficiency**: How Ghostty handles scrollback (vs WezTerm's VecDeque<Line>)
2. **I/O pipeline**: How Ghostty reads PTY output (vs WezTerm's 2-thread-per-pane model)
3. **Event system**: How Ghostty handles notifications (vs WezTerm's RwLock fan-out)
4. **Configuration**: How Ghostty handles runtime config changes
5. **Mux architecture**: If/how Ghostty does multiplexing (vs WezTerm's singleton Mux)

## Workflow per finding
1. Study the Zig code and document WHAT it does and WHY (not how the Zig implements it)
2. Write a language-neutral specification of the algorithm/pattern
3. Invoke /porting-to-rust with the spec
4. Implement in idiomatic, memory-safe Rust
5. Write comprehensive tests
6. Credit Ghostty in commit messages where design was inspired

## Sub-beads
1. Clone and inventory — get the codebase, map the architecture
2. Memory architecture analysis — scrollback, buffers, allocation patterns
3. I/O pipeline analysis — PTY reading, parsing, rendering pipeline
4. Event/notification system analysis — how changes propagate
5. Synthesis report — comparison doc with actionable FrankenTerm improvements

## Ethical Considerations
- Ghostty is open source (MIT license) — studying is fine
- We're extracting IDEAS and PATTERNS via clean room methodology
- All code will be original idiomatic Rust, written from specs not from Zig source
- Credit Ghostty in commit messages where appropriate","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2026-02-09T19:37:22.248449Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-02-10T15:55:57.503968Z"}
 {"id":"wa-3bja.1","title":"Clone Ghostty and create architectural inventory","description":"## Goal
Clone the Ghostty repository into ~/projects/legacy_ghostty_code/ and create an architectural inventory: key files, module structure, dependencies, and high-level design patterns.

## Steps
1. `git clone https://github.com/ghostty-org/ghostty.git ~/projects/legacy_ghostty_code`
2. Map the directory structure and identify key modules
3. Read the README, ARCHITECTURE.md, or equivalent design docs
4. Identify the main subsystems: terminal emulation, rendering, I/O, config, mux (if any)
5. Create an inventory document: ~/projects/legacy_ghostty_code/INVENTORY.md

## Key Questions to Answer
- What language(s) is Ghostty written in? (Zig + some C)
- How is the codebase organized? (src/, lib/, etc.)
- What are the main entry points?
- How does it handle terminal emulation vs rendering vs I/O?
- Does it have any multiplexing/session management?
- What third-party dependencies does it use?
- How does it handle cross-platform (macOS, Linux)?

## Acceptance Criteria
- Repository cloned successfully
- INVENTORY.md created with architectural overview
- Key files identified for deep analysis in subsequent beads","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-09T19:37:32.578823Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-02-09T19:37:32.578823Z","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"wa-3bja.1","depends_on_id":"wa-3bja","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-09T19:37:32.578823Z","created_by":"jemanuel"}]}
 {"id":"wa-3bja.2","title":"Ghostty memory architecture analysis — scrollback, cells, allocation","description":"## Goal
Analyze Ghostty's memory management architecture, focusing on how it handles scrollback buffers, terminal cell storage, and memory lifecycle — then compare with WezTerm's approach to identify improvement opportunities for FrankenTerm.

## Analysis Focus Areas

### Scrollback Storage
- How does Ghostty store scrollback lines? (WezTerm: VecDeque<Line>, 100% in-memory)
- Does it use disk paging, mmap, or ring buffers?
- What's the per-cell memory overhead? (WezTerm: ~24 bytes/cell)
- How does it handle scrollback limits and eviction?

### Terminal Cell Representation
- How are cells with attributes stored? (WezTerm: Cell struct with CellAttributes)
- Is there any cell deduplication or compression?
- How are wide characters (CJK) handled?
- How are hyperlinks stored?

### Allocation Patterns
- Does Ghostty use arena allocators or custom allocators?
- How does it handle allocation/deallocation of pane state?
- Are there any "never shrink" antipatterns like WezTerm's HashMap caches?

### Memory Pressure Handling
- Does Ghostty monitor memory usage?
- Does it have any eviction or GC mechanisms?
- How does it handle OOM situations?

## Deliverable
A comparison document identifying 3-5 concrete ideas FrankenTerm could adopt:
- Pattern name
- Ghostty approach (with file references)
- WezTerm's current approach
- FrankenTerm opportunity (what we'd change and expected impact)

## Dependencies
- Clone and inventory (sub-task 1)

## Acceptance Criteria
- Analysis covers all 4 focus areas
- At least 3 actionable improvement ideas identified
- Each idea has clear implementation path for FrankenTerm","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-09T19:37:42.631782Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-02-09T19:38:12.37304Z","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"wa-3bja.2","depends_on_id":"wa-3bja","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-09T19:37:42.631782Z","created_by":"jemanuel"},{"issue_id":"wa-3bja.2","depends_on_id":"wa-3bja.1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-09T19:38:12.372763Z","created_by":"jemanuel"}]}
 {"id":"wa-3bja.3","title":"Ghostty I/O pipeline analysis — PTY reading, parsing, rendering","description":"## Goal
Analyze Ghostty's I/O pipeline — how it reads PTY output, parses terminal escape sequences, and renders — then compare with WezTerm's 2-thread-per-pane model to identify performance improvement opportunities for FrankenTerm.

## Analysis Focus Areas

### PTY Reading
- How does Ghostty read from PTY file descriptors? (WezTerm: dedicated read_from_pane_pty thread per pane)
- Does it use epoll/kqueue multiplexing?
- Does it use a thread pool or async I/O?
- What buffer sizes does it use?

### Parsing Pipeline
- How does Ghostty parse VT escape sequences? (WezTerm: dedicated parse_buffered_data thread per pane)
- Is parsing done in the same thread as reading?
- How does it handle parser state across chunks?
- Is the parser zero-copy?

### Rendering Pipeline
- How does Ghostty decide what to render after a parse?
- Is rendering batched or immediate?
- How does it handle rapid output (e.g., `cat large_file.txt`)?
- Does it coalesce rendering updates?

### Thread Model
- Total thread count for N panes? (WezTerm: 2N threads)
- How does it scale with many panes?
- Does it use work-stealing or fixed thread pools?

## Deliverable
Comparison document with 3-5 ideas for FrankenTerm's capture pipeline:
- How FrankenTerm's tailer/capture could adopt Ghostty's I/O patterns
- Potential thread count reduction
- Potential latency improvements

## Dependencies
- Clone and inventory (sub-task 1)

## Acceptance Criteria
- Analysis covers all 4 focus areas
- At least 3 actionable improvement ideas
- Each idea maps to specific FrankenTerm code (tailer.rs, mux_client.rs, etc.)","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-09T19:37:52.050787Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-02-09T19:38:12.505399Z","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"wa-3bja.3","depends_on_id":"wa-3bja","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-09T19:37:52.050787Z","created_by":"jemanuel"},{"issue_id":"wa-3bja.3","depends_on_id":"wa-3bja.1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-09T19:38:12.505332Z","created_by":"jemanuel"}]}
 {"id":"wa-3bja.4","title":"Ghostty event system analysis — change propagation and notifications","description":"## Goal
Analyze Ghostty's event and notification system — how terminal state changes propagate to the UI and other consumers — then compare with WezTerm's RwLock-based notification fan-out to find better patterns for FrankenTerm.

## Analysis Focus Areas

### Change Propagation
- How does Ghostty notify the renderer that cells have changed? (WezTerm: Screen::set_dirty() + RwLock)
- Is it event-based, polling-based, or dirty-flag-based?
- How granular are change notifications? (per-cell, per-line, per-region, per-screen?)

### Lock Contention
- What synchronization primitives does Ghostty use?
- Does it avoid the "single lock for all panes" problem WezTerm has?
- How does it handle concurrent reads (rendering) and writes (PTY output)?

### Notification Batching
- Does Ghostty batch notifications?
- How does it handle notification storms during rapid output?
- Is there any coalescing or debouncing?

## Deliverable
Comparison document identifying patterns FrankenTerm could adopt in its capture pipeline to reduce lock contention and notification overhead.

## Dependencies
- Clone and inventory (sub-task 1)

## Acceptance Criteria
- Analysis covers change propagation, locking, and batching
- At least 2 actionable ideas for FrankenTerm","status":"open","priority":3,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-09T19:37:59.365549Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-02-09T19:38:12.619693Z","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"wa-3bja.4","depends_on_id":"wa-3bja","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-09T19:37:59.365549Z","created_by":"jemanuel"},{"issue_id":"wa-3bja.4","depends_on_id":"wa-3bja.1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-09T19:38:12.619595Z","created_by":"jemanuel"}]}
-{"id":"wa-3bja.5","title":"Ghostty analysis synthesis — comparison report and improvement roadmap","description":"## Goal
Synthesize all Ghostty analysis findings into a comprehensive comparison report with prioritized, actionable improvement recommendations for FrankenTerm.

## Report Structure
1. **Executive Summary**: Top 5 ideas ranked by impact/effort ratio
2. **Memory**: Ghostty vs WezTerm vs FrankenTerm opportunity
3. **I/O Pipeline**: Thread model comparison, pipeline design
4. **Event System**: Notification/change propagation comparison
5. **Architecture Patterns**: General design patterns worth adopting
6. **Implementation Roadmap**: Ordered list of improvements with dependencies

## Deliverable
- docs/ghostty-analysis-synthesis.md in the FrankenTerm repo
- New beads created for each top-5 improvement idea
- Dependency links to existing performance beads where they overlap

## Dependencies
- All analysis sub-tasks (memory, I/O, event, mux)

## Acceptance Criteria
- Report covers all analysis areas
- Top 5 ideas have clear implementation paths
- New beads created for ideas not already covered
- Cross-referenced with existing performance improvement beads","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-09T19:38:06.395234Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-02-09T19:38:12.949927Z","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"wa-3bja.5","depends_on_id":"wa-3bja","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-09T19:38:06.395234Z","created_by":"jemanuel"},{"issue_id":"wa-3bja.5","depends_on_id":"wa-3bja.2","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-09T19:38:12.734882Z","created_by":"jemanuel"},{"issue_id":"wa-3bja.5","depends_on_id":"wa-3bja.3","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-09T19:38:12.835922Z","created_by":"jemanuel"},{"issue_id":"wa-3bja.5","depends_on_id":"wa-3bja.4","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-09T19:38:12.949848Z","created_by":"jemanuel"}]}
+{"id":"wa-3bja.5","title":"Ghostty analysis synthesis — comparison report and improvement roadmap","description":"# Ghostty Analysis Synthesis — Comparison Report and Improvement Roadmap

## Goal
Synthesize all Ghostty analysis findings into a comprehensive comparison report with prioritized, actionable improvement recommendations for FrankenTerm.

## Deliverable: Three-tier recommendation report

### 1. Quick wins (adopt immediately)
- What Ghostty does and why it's good
- Language-neutral spec of the pattern
- Estimated effort to implement in FrankenTerm
- Related existing beads

### 2. Strategic improvements (worth investing in)
- Architecture sketch in Rust terms
- Migration path from current WezTerm patterns
- New beads to create

### 3. Future considerations (track but defer)
- Why it's interesting
- What would need to change first

## CRITICAL: /porting-to-rust skill requirement
Any implementation beads created from this synthesis MUST specify that the /porting-to-rust skill is required. The workflow is:
1. This synthesis produces language-neutral specs of each recommended pattern
2. Implementation beads reference those specs
3. Agents invoke /porting-to-rust to produce idiomatic, memory-safe Rust
4. NO line-by-line Zig translation — clean room only

## Cross-references
- wa-3kxe (Fork Hardening)
- wa-3cyp (Performance Optimization)
- wa-rsaf (Session Persistence)
- wa-e34d9 (asupersync migration)

## Acceptance criteria
- All analysis beads synthesized
- 5+ actionable recommendations with language-neutral specs
- Each recommendation specifies /porting-to-rust as implementation methodology
- New beads created for recommendations not covered by existing work","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-09T19:38:06.395234Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-02-10T15:56:15.833632Z","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"wa-3bja.5","depends_on_id":"wa-3bja","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-09T19:38:06.395234Z","created_by":"jemanuel"},{"issue_id":"wa-3bja.5","depends_on_id":"wa-3bja.2","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-09T19:38:12.734882Z","created_by":"jemanuel"},{"issue_id":"wa-3bja.5","depends_on_id":"wa-3bja.3","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-09T19:38:12.835922Z","created_by":"jemanuel"},{"issue_id":"wa-3bja.5","depends_on_id":"wa-3bja.4","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-09T19:38:12.949848Z","created_by":"jemanuel"}]}
 {"id":"wa-3cso","title":"FTUI-03.2 Enforce one-writer output routing for UI and log surfaces","description":"## Background\nfrankentui requires a one-writer rule. wa must eliminate direct stdout/stderr writes from active UI runtime paths.\n\n## Deliverables\n- unified output sink routing UI redraw + logs\n- explicit policy for subprocess output capture/routing\n- violation detection strategy in tests/CI\n\n## Acceptance Criteria\n- no direct terminal writes bypass output gate in migrated paths\n- sustained output does not corrupt cursor/layout state.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"LavenderGrove","created_at":"2026-02-08T20:07:51.521424086Z","created_by":"GrayHarbor","updated_at":"2026-02-09T01:44:58.629048059Z","closed_at":"2026-02-09T01:44:58.628915122Z","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"wa-3cso","depends_on_id":"wa-1brb","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-08T20:07:51.534554022Z","created_by":"GrayHarbor"},{"issue_id":"wa-3cso","depends_on_id":"wa-2qyt","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-08T20:16:24.23128338Z","created_by":"GrayHarbor"}]}
 {"id":"wa-3cyp","title":"[EPIC] WezTerm Performance Optimization for Agent Swarms","description":"## Goal
Optimize WezTerm + wa for intensive AI agent swarm workloads: 50+ concurrent panes on 256GB RAM / 32+ core servers, running 24/7 with Claude Code, Codex, and Gemini sessions.

## Background & Motivation
We observed severe WezTerm degradation under agent swarm workloads:
- **Memory**: 76GB RSS after 23 days (VecDeque<Line> in-memory scrollback, HashMap caches never shrink, ~5MB thread accumulation per pane spawn/terminate)
- **CPU**: 35-40% with 24+ panes (2 threads per pane, zstd compression on local socket, Lua callbacks in hot path: format-tab-title invoked 3714x per alert)
- **Stuck processes**: 60+ zombie `wezterm cli` processes from lock contention on Mux singleton, synchronous PDU protocol with no timeout
- **Protocol corruption**: UnitResponse when expecting ListPanesResponse, feedback loops generating 150K log lines in 20 seconds

These are wa-side improvements that work around WezTerm's limitations without forking WezTerm itself. They make wa resilient against the known failure modes.

## Sub-beads (Priority Order)
1. Connection pool for DirectMuxClient (bd-41w) — eliminate CLI subprocess spawning
2. CLI command timeout hardening (bd-6js) — kill_on_drop and orphan reaper
3. Tiered update rates (bd-9dp) — aggressive backoff for idle panes
4. Protocol error auto-recovery — reconnect on UnexpectedResponse
5. Mux server watchdog — health monitoring and auto-restart
6. Scrollback memory pressure mitigation — evict old content under pressure
7. Content-addressable output dedup — deduplicate repetitive agent output

## Architecture
These improvements layer on top of wa's existing infrastructure:
- Pool<C> (pool.rs) — generic connection pool with health checks
- DegradationManager (degradation.rs) — graceful degradation subsystem
- BackpressureMonitor (backpressure.rs) — Green/Yellow/Red/Black tiers
- HeartbeatRegistry (watchdog.rs) — health monitoring
- StorageManager (storage.rs) — SQLite with content_hash support

## Acceptance Criteria
- wa handles 50+ panes without stuck processes
- Memory growth bounded (wa-side, can't fix WezTerm's scrollback)
- Protocol errors recovered automatically
- Mux server health monitored with alerts","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2026-02-09T19:33:55.401606Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-02-09T19:33:55.401606Z"}
 {"id":"wa-3cyp.1","title":"Performance benchmark suite — criterion benchmarks, stress tests, regression tracking","description":"## Goal
Build a criterion-based benchmark suite that quantifies every performance improvement, providing irrefutable before/after measurements with statistical rigor. Also includes a stress test harness for long-running stability validation.

## Background & Motivation
The performance optimization epic (wa-3cyp) makes claims like "80-95% reduction in capture operations" and "10-50x latency reduction." Without benchmarks, these are guesses. We need:
1. **Baseline measurements** before implementing any changes
2. **Per-improvement benchmarks** that prove each change helps
3. **Regression prevention** — benchmarks run in CI to catch slowdowns
4. **Stress tests** — 24h+ runs that verify no memory leaks or degradation

## Benchmark Modules

### 1. Connection Pool Benchmarks (for bd-41w)
**File**: `crates/wa-core/benches/connection_pool.rs`
```rust
// Baseline: spawn wezterm CLI subprocess per operation
fn bench_cli_subprocess_overhead(c: &mut Criterion)
// Improved: connection pool with pre-established connections  
fn bench_pool_get_connection(c: &mut Criterion)
// Comparison: 50 sequential operations pool vs subprocess
fn bench_50_sequential_operations(c: &mut Criterion)
// Concurrency: 50 concurrent operations through pool
fn bench_50_concurrent_pool_operations(c: &mut Criterion)
// Pool health: connection health check latency
fn bench_pool_health_check(c: &mut Criterion)
```

### 2. CLI Timeout Benchmarks (for bd-6js)
**File**: `crates/wa-core/benches/cli_timeout.rs`
```rust
// Timeout enforcement: kill_on_drop triggers within deadline
fn bench_timeout_enforcement_latency(c: &mut Criterion)
// Orphan reaper: detect and kill zombie processes
fn bench_orphan_detection_speed(c: &mut Criterion)
// Normal operation: overhead added by timeout wrapper
fn bench_timeout_wrapper_overhead(c: &mut Criterion)
```

### 3. Capture Pipeline Benchmarks (for bd-9dp, wa-x4rq)
**File**: `crates/wa-core/benches/capture_pipeline.rs`
```rust
// Baseline: capture all 50 panes at uniform rate
fn bench_uniform_capture_50_panes(c: &mut Criterion)
// Tiered: capture with idle/active/background classification
fn bench_tiered_capture_50_panes(c: &mut Criterion)
// Coalescing: notification burst → coalesced captures
fn bench_notification_coalescing_burst(c: &mut Criterion)
// Throughput: max panes capturable per second
fn bench_capture_throughput(c: &mut Criterion)
```

### 4. Scrollback Benchmarks (for wa-3r5e, wa-8vla)
**File**: `crates/wa-core/benches/scrollback.rs`
```rust
// Memory: scrollback for 50 panes x 10K lines
fn bench_scrollback_memory_footprint(c: &mut Criterion)
// Pressure: eviction under memory pressure
fn bench_scrollback_eviction_latency(c: &mut Criterion)
// Dedup: content-addressable dedup savings ratio
fn bench_scrollback_dedup_ratio(c: &mut Criterion)
// mmap: mmap-backed read vs SQLite read for 10K lines
fn bench_mmap_vs_sqlite_read(c: &mut Criterion)
```

### 5. Protocol & Encoding Benchmarks (for wa-29cb, wa-2c50)
**File**: `crates/wa-core/benches/protocol.rs`
```rust
// zstd: compression overhead for typical PDU sizes
fn bench_zstd_compress_typical_pdu(c: &mut Criterion)
// Identity: no-compression for local socket
fn bench_identity_codec_throughput(c: &mut Criterion)
// Recovery: time to detect and recover from protocol error
fn bench_protocol_error_recovery(c: &mut Criterion)
```

### 6. Cache GC Benchmarks (for wa-1nkt)
**File**: `crates/wa-core/benches/cache_gc.rs`
```rust
// GC cycle: time to shrink caches with 1000 dead entries
fn bench_gc_cycle_1000_dead_entries(c: &mut Criterion)
// GC impact: cache lookup latency before vs after GC
fn bench_cache_lookup_pre_post_gc(c: &mut Criterion)
// SQLite VACUUM: time for VACUUM on 100MB database
fn bench_sqlite_vacuum_100mb(c: &mut Criterion)
```

### 7. Batch Query Benchmarks (for wa-11zm)
**File**: `crates/wa-core/benches/batch_queries.rs`
```rust
// Sequential: 50 individual get-text calls
fn bench_sequential_50_get_text(c: &mut Criterion)
// Batch: single batch get-text for 50 panes
fn bench_batch_50_get_text(c: &mut Criterion)
// TOON: batch output in TOON vs JSON encoding
fn bench_batch_toon_vs_json(c: &mut Criterion)
```

## Stress Test Harness
**File**: `crates/wa-core/tests/stress/mod.rs`

Long-running tests (not run in CI, run manually):
```rust
// 24h stability: capture pipeline with 50 mock panes, verify no memory growth
#[test] #[ignore] fn stress_24h_capture_stability()
// Memory leak detection: capture + GC for 10K iterations, assert RSS stable
#[test] #[ignore] fn stress_memory_leak_detection()
// Connection pool: 100K get/release cycles, verify no leaked connections
#[test] #[ignore] fn stress_pool_100k_cycles()
// Protocol recovery: inject 1000 random errors, verify all recovered
#[test] #[ignore] fn stress_protocol_recovery_1000_errors()
```

## Logging & Reporting
- Each benchmark emits structured JSON to `target/criterion/` (criterion default)
- Stress tests log per-iteration metrics to `target/stress-logs/`:
  ```json
  {"iteration": 42000, "rss_mb": 145, "panes": 50, "captures_per_sec": 234, "pool_connections": 8, "gc_bytes_freed": 1024000}
  ```
- CI integration: `cargo bench -- --output-format=bencher` for GitHub Actions benchmark tracking
- Regression detection: criterion's built-in comparison with ±5% threshold

## Infrastructure
```rust
// crates/wa-core/benches/helpers.rs
pub fn mock_mux_server(pane_count: usize) -> MockMuxServer { /* ... */ }
pub fn generate_scrollback(lines: usize, width: usize) -> Vec<String> { /* ... */ }
pub fn with_temp_db<F: FnOnce(&Connection)>(f: F) { /* ... */ }
```

## Dependencies
- Should be created early (before implementations start) so baselines can be captured
- Uses the same MockMuxClient as the unit test suite

## Acceptance Criteria
- 25+ benchmarks across 7 modules
- Baselines captured before any optimization work begins
- `cargo bench` runs all benchmarks in < 60 seconds (excluding stress tests)
- Criterion HTML reports generated in target/criterion/
- Stress tests available via `cargo test --test stress -- --ignored`
- CI integration for regression detection
- JSON-structured logging for all stress tests","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-09T20:03:42.602108Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-02-09T20:04:27.465314Z","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"wa-3cyp.1","depends_on_id":"wa-3cyp","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-09T20:03:42.602108Z","created_by":"jemanuel"},{"issue_id":"wa-3cyp.1","depends_on_id":"bd-41w","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-09T20:04:27.465226Z","created_by":"jemanuel"}]}
